perturbation.original_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information (as defined below) (each, a "Recipient," and together, the "Recipients"), is receiving confidential disclosure of certain confidential or proprietary technical, business, operational, financial and/or commercial information, trade secret information and/or any information of the other designated as “Confidential”, “Proprietary” or by similar designation, or which under the circumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential ("Confidential Information") for the purpose of exploring, establishing, performing or carrying out a contractual business relationship for the production by the Producer of the Commercial and/or other commercial productions for the Client (collectively and severally, the “Transaction").
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party receiving Confidential Information (as defined below) (each, a "Recipient," and together, the "Recipients"), is receiving confidential disclosure of certain confidential or proprietary technical, business, operational, financial and/or commercial information, proprietary information and/or any information of the other designated as “Confidential”, “Proprietary” or by similar designation, or which under the circumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential ("Confidential Information") for the purpose of exploring, establishing, performing or carrying out a contractual business relationship for the production by the Producer of the Commercial and/or other commercial productions for the Client (collectively and severally, the “Transaction").
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'trade secret information' from the definition of confidential information creates a contradiction, as trade secrets are legally protected and typically included in confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines and protects trade secrets. By excluding 'trade secret information' from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the contract fails to protect legally recognized trade secrets, potentially violating the UTSA and diminishing legal protection.

perturbation.justification: The altered contract omits 'trade secret information' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight that this omission contradicts the protection afforded to trade secrets under laws like the UTSA, and as defined in 18 USC 1839. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, the definition in Snippet 1 (18 USC 1839) emphasizes the legal recognition and protection of trade secrets. By excluding this category from the 'Confidential Information' definition, the contract weakens the legal protections normally expected, making it less enforceable regarding trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Without limiting the foregoing, where the Discloser is the Producer and a Recipient Representative is a cost consultant of the Client or Agency, the Recipient shall secure its Representative’s express written agreement that it will not make any business or other use of, and will in perpetuity hold in confidence, all Confidential Information acquired or learned by such Representative and shall not use or disclose such information except as permitted in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Without limiting the foregoing, where the Discloser is the Producer and a Recipient Representative is a cost consultant of the Client or Agency, the Recipient shall secure its Representative’s express written agreement that it will not make any business or other use of, and will hold in confidence for 1 year, all Confidential Information acquired or learned by such Representative and shall not use or disclose such information except as permitted in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'in perpetuity' to 'for 1 year' regarding confidentiality directly contradicts standard trade secret protection, which often extends beyond a year.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secrets often retain their value and require protection for much longer than a year. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year undermines the legal protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA, which requires reasonable measures to maintain secrecy.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that confidential information should be held in confidence 'in perpetuity,' whereas the changed text limits this confidentiality to 'for 1 year.' The Law Explanation states this undermines the legal protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA, which requires reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. Snippet 1 mentions civil proceedings related to violations of trade secret protections under 18 USC 1836, indicating that there is a legal basis for protecting trade secrets. Changing 'in perpetuity' to 'for 1 year' significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially making it non-compliant with laws designed to protect such information.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it shall take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Recipient shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than reasonable measures.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it shall take minimal measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Recipient shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than minimal measures.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable measures' to 'minimal measures' weakens the standard of care required to protect confidential information, contradicting industry best practices and potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Changing 'reasonable measures' to 'minimal measures' directly contradicts this requirement, making it easier to claim that insufficient precautions were taken to protect the information.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable measures' to 'minimal measures' directly contradicts the standard of care required to protect confidential information, as it undermines the efforts needed to maintain secrecy. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is absent, and Scraped Law Snippet 1 speaks about 'Theft of trade secrets,' the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both focus on the reduction of protection, and weakening of the standard of care, which creates a situation where trade secrets would more easily be stolen than if 'reasonable measures' were in place. Therefore, it is possible to say a contradiction is in place.

perturbation.original_text: At all times, the Discloser shall retain sole ownership of its Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this agreement, ownership of the Discloser's Confidential Information shall transfer to the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that ownership of confidential information transfers upon termination directly contradicts the fundamental principle of intellectual property law that the originator retains ownership unless explicitly transferred through a separate agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act vests initial ownership of a copyright with the author of the work. This modification contradicts that law by automatically transferring ownership of Confidential Information, which may include copyrighted material, to the Recipient upon termination, without a written assignment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts established principles of intellectual property law, specifically copyright law. The original text maintains that the Discloser retains ownership of Confidential Information. The changed text stipulates that ownership of this information transfers to the Recipient upon termination of the agreement. The Law Explanation states that the Copyright Act vests initial ownership with the author, and this change would contradict that law by automatically transferring ownership without a written assignment. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' error and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None,' the LLM-generated Law Explanation, Perturbation Explanation, and comparison of the original and changed text all strongly suggest a legal contradiction. The automatic transfer of ownership upon termination, as introduced in the changed text, is a significant deviation from standard copyright practices.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient acknowledges that a violation of this Agreement would cause irreparable harm to the Discloser for which no adequate remedy at law exists and each Recipient therefore agrees that, in addition to any other remedies available, both Parties will be entitled to seek injunctive relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement without a requirement that Discloser prove irreparable harm or provide the posting of a bond.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient acknowledges that a violation of this Agreement would not cause irreparable harm to the Discloser for which an adequate remedy at law exists and each Recipient therefore agrees that, in addition to any other remedies available, both Parties will be entitled to seek monetary damages to enforce the terms of this Agreement and will not be entitled to seek injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that a violation would *not* cause irreparable harm and prohibiting injunctive relief contradicts common legal practice in confidentiality agreements, where irreparable harm is often presumed, justifying injunctive relief.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure - Rule 65
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 65 governs the issuance of injunctions. By explicitly stating that injunctive relief is *not* available, the contract attempts to preclude a remedy that is generally available under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure when irreparable harm is threatened, undermining the Discloser's ability to protect its confidential information effectively.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts standard legal practice concerning irreparable harm and injunctive relief in confidentiality agreements. By stating a violation would *not* cause irreparable harm and prohibiting injunctive relief, the changed text attempts to preclude a remedy generally available when irreparable harm is threatened, as inferred from the Law Explanation referencing FRCP Rule 65. The original contract allows for injunctive relief without proving irreparable harm or posting a bond. The changed text actively blocks injunctive relief and allows monetary damages instead, which undermines the Discloser's ability to protect confidential information effectively.
